title: Key Management, Rotation, HSMs, KMS, and Compromise · Go Build Room
summary: A product-security pair incrementally builds key lifecycle, custody, and rotation runbook, changes one attacker-controlled input, and keeps earlier Go, accessibility, secret-lifecycle, testing, and evidence invariants active. Planned artifact: Build a complete first version of key lifecycle, custody, and rotation runbook while every earlier competency remains active. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Define generation, registration, activation, distribution, use, suspension, rotation, archival, recovery, revocation, destruction, and evidence ownership.
Separate immutable key version, stable logical key ID, mutable alias, algorithm, purpose, state, and cryptoperiod.
Grant narrow sign, verify, encrypt, decrypt, wrap, unwrap, derive, rotate, export, and administer capabilities across separate identities.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Cryptography and Go Commit to the mission model · Workshop Crypto Go Key Lifecycle
steps.instruction:
workshop case — A workshop cryptographic engineering team handles case 1348 during a credential migration window. Build deterministic Go evidence to enforce immutable key identity, state, cryptoperiod, least privilege, and tested recovery; cap bytes, work, memory, time, attempts, concurrency, and retained secret material before use; then change one authenticated byte and prove clean rejection without partial effect. Browser code uses pure state models or the narrow side-effect-free crypto allowlist with fixed fixtures. Production entropy and keys, secret custody, native timing and side channels, networks, TLS, HSM/KMS, certificate systems, FIPS or compliance state, load, faults, restore, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Define generation, registration, activation, distribution, use, suspension, rotation, archival, recovery, revocation, destruction, and evidence ownership.

What must be established before implementation?

Evidence target: Cryptography and Go Commit to the mission model · Workshop Crypto Go Key Lifecycle.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for define generation, registration, activation, distribution, use, suspension, rotation, archival, recovery, revocation, destruction, and evidence ownership. For Cryptography and Go Commit to the mission model · Workshop Crypto Go Key Lifecycle, keep Crypto Go Key Lifecycle connected to executable Go behavior evidence.

Key management begins and ends with generating random bytes. That claim does not survive the commit to the mission model evidence boundary.

For commit to the mission model, run one deterministic Crypto Go Key Lifecycle model, omit canonical bytes, asset, adversary, key and nonce ownership, tamper rejection, secret-safe failure, and native transfer gates, then call the browser result cryptographically secure.

steps.title: Cryptography and Go Crypto Go Key Lifecycle · changed case · Workshop Crypto Go Key Lifecycle
steps.instruction:
workshop case — A workshop cryptographic engineering team handles case 6205 during a certificate rollover incident. Build deterministic Go evidence to enforce immutable key identity, state, cryptoperiod, least privilege, and tested recovery; retain exact canonical bytes, purpose, role, version, and artifact identity; then rotate the active key while old data remains readable and new writes cannot downgrade. Browser code uses pure state models or the narrow side-effect-free crypto allowlist with fixed fixtures. Production entropy and keys, secret custody, native timing and side channels, networks, TLS, HSM/KMS, certificate systems, FIPS or compliance state, load, faults, restore, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.
